channel.title: Drupal Core
channel.description: Stay informed about developments in Drupal Core.

item.title: #3605262: Database write optimizations result in inconsistent data in data and revision tables
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Critical task
  • Version: 11.4.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 2 files, +22/−27 · php +22/−27
  • Diff: a3e9247
  • Discussion: #3605262 · 3 contributors · 10 comments
  • Followers: 6

Summary

A critical data-integrity regression introduced by the database write optimizations in #3554579 has been reverted. When an existing pending revision was promoted to the default revision while field values were also changed in the same save call, the revision table was not updated, leaving it out of sync with the base table. This could cause silent data loss or inconsistency for sites using Content Moderation, Workspaces, or any contributed module that promotes revisions and modifies fields simultaneously. The fix reverts the optimization in SqlContentEntityStorage::saveToDedicatedTables() and adds a targeted regression test. The original issue (#3554579) has been re-opened to attempt a corrected optimization.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The optimization in #3554579 introduced three local variables in saveToDedicatedTables(): $save_to_base_table, $enforce_save_to_base_table, and $save_to_revision_table. The intent was to skip the revision-table write when promoting a pending revision to default (because only the base table was assumed to need updating). However, the condition set $save_to_revision_table = false whenever $enforce_save_to_base_table was true, regardless of whether field values had also changed. When field values did change in the same operation, those new values were written only to the base table, leaving the revision table holding stale data.

The revert removes all three variables and restores simpler, always-write logic: the base table is written when $entity->isDefaultRevision() returns true; the revision table is written whenever $this->entityType->isRevisionable() returns true. The skip-on-no-change guard is simplified to: skip only when not a new revision, the default-revision flag has not changed ($entity->isDefaultRevision() === $original->isDefaultRevision()), and hasFieldValueChanged() returns false.

The regression test added to testFieldEntityRevisionWrite() exercises the precise failing scenario: saving a pending revision with a new value (baz), then loading by both loadUnchanged() and loadRevision() to confirm both the base and revision tables reflect the new value.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: June 21, 2026
  • Committed: June 23, 2026 (2 days and 3 commits later)

Key contributors

  • berdir (MD Systems GmbH)
  • catch (Tag1 Consulting, Inc.)

Collaboration

berdir opened the issue and merge request immediately after spotting failures in the entity_reference_revisions test suite, identifying the exact trigger as simultaneously changing the default-revision flag and field values on an existing revision. catch commented that reverting first and re-opening #3554579 for a corrected attempt was the right path, and sketched two potential approaches: either deprecating the mixed-operation pattern (if detectable) or accounting for it within the optimization itself.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3591520: Fix format of some deprecation messages
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Major bug report
  • Version: 11.4.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 22 files, +35/−35 · php +35/−35
  • Diff: af90abb
  • Discussion: #3591520 · 7 contributors · 26 comments
  • Followers: 7

Summary

Several deprecation and exception messages in Drupal core were missing the () suffix on function and method names, and one message was also missing a leading space, producing malformed strings like "__FUNCTION__is deprecated". These have been corrected across multiple modules so that all @trigger_error deprecation notices and BadMethodCallException messages follow the standard PHP convention of including () after the callable name.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The problem was found while reviewing deprecation messages consumed by automated tooling such as Rector and the Drupal update bot. Three categories of mistakes were fixed in the same commit:

  1. __FUNCTION__ . 'is deprecated (missing both () and a space before "is") in locale.module.
  2. __FUNCTION__ . ' is deprecated and __METHOD__ . ' is deprecated (space present but no ()) throughout the codebase.
  3. __METHOD__ . ' is not allowed' in BadMethodCallException throws inside CheckpointStorage and ReadOnlyStorage.

Affected files span core/modules/config (CheckpointStorage, ReadOnlyStorage), core/lib/Drupal/Core/Entity (EntityType, SqlContentEntityStorage), core/lib/Drupal/Core/ImageToolkit (ImageToolkitOperationBase, ImageToolkitOperationManager), core/lib/Drupal/Core/Recipe (RecipeRunner), core/lib/Drupal/Core/Routing (RouteBuilder), core/lib/Drupal/Core/Theme (ThemeAccessCheck), core/lib/Drupal/Core/Utility (Error), and the file, locale, taxonomy, user, and views modules. Corresponding test assertions (expectUserDeprecationMessage, assertEquals on exception text) were updated to match the corrected strings.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: May 22, 2026
  • Committed: June 23, 2026 (1 month and 3 commits later)

Key contributors

  • bbrala (SWIS)
  • quietone (PreviousNext)
  • dcam

Collaboration

During the review, dcam ran targeted grep searches against both main and 11.x branches and found five additional instances of missing-space that the original MR had not addressed, prompting a fix. Later, quietone expanded the grep patterns to also cover __CLASS__ usages and ran a shell script against both fix branches to confirm no further occurrences remained before the final RTBC.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3552669: Error when fetching all query results as class instances
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Major bug report
  • Version: 11.4.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 2 files, +40/−1 · php +40/−1
  • Diff: eeb5c18
  • Discussion: #3552669 · 8 contributors · 43 comments
  • Followers: 11

Summary

Calling fetchAll() on a database query result when the fetch mode was set to a custom class (either via the fetch query option or via setFetchMode(FetchAs::ClassObject, ...)) threw a TypeError at runtime: PDOStatement::fetchAll(): Argument #2 must be of type string, int given. This is now fixed, and query results are correctly returned as instances of the requested class.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The bug lived in PdoResult::fetchAll(). Its original implementation used a null-coalescing chain to resolve the second argument passed to clientFetchAll():

$this->clientFetchAll($mode, $fetchOptions['column'] ?? $fetchOptions['class'] ?? NULL, ...)

StatementBase initialises $fetchOptions['column'] to integer 0 by default. Because 0 is not null, the ?? operator short-circuits and the class name stored in $fetchOptions['class'] is never reached. clientFetchAll therefore received 0 (an integer) instead of a class-name string, triggering the TypeError from PDO.

The fix replaces the coalescing chain with a match expression keyed on the active FetchAs enum case:

return $this->clientFetchAll($mode, ...match ($mode) {
  FetchAs::Column => [$fetchOptions['column'] ?? NULL],
  FetchAs::ClassObject => [
    $fetchOptions['class'] ?? NULL,
    $fetchOptions['constructor_args'] ?? NULL,
  ],
  default => [],
});

This ensures each fetch mode receives only its own relevant arguments, regardless of whatever default values are present in $fetchOptions. Two new kernel tests were added to FetchTest: testQueryFetchAllAsClassInstances (covering the fetch query option path) and testQueryFetchAllAsFetchModeClassInstances (covering setFetchMode with constructor arguments). The change was also backported to 11.x and 11.4.x.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: October 16, 2025
  • Committed: June 23, 2026 (8 months and 3 commits later)

Key contributors

  • pjotr.savitski
  • quietone (PreviousNext)
  • godotislate
  • smustgrave (Mobomo)
  • rhezios
  • alexpott (Acro Commerce)
  • dpi (PreviousNext)
  • mondrake

Collaboration

The issue went through two distinct implementation approaches before landing. The original submitter (pjotr.savitski) identified the root cause and proposed a fix in StatementBase::setFetchMode() that would explicitly null out the column key when switching to ClassObject mode. A second contributor (rhezios) noted that this second approach broke cases with constructor arguments and instead implemented the first proposed option (nulling the column) in a separate MR. Then alexpott suggested moving the fix entirely to PdoResult::fetchAll() as a more robust and centralised solution. dpi picked up that suggestion, implemented it, and noted in the thread that the Entity Hierarchy module had already encountered the same breakage as a downstream consequence of #3522561. mondrake reviewed and requested minor test consistency improvements, which dpi addressed before the patch was committed.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3496328: Use a hash of the cache data for CacheCollector race condition detection
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Major task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 8 files, +349/−94 · php +349/−94
  • Diff: 35cf89b
  • Discussion: #3496328 · 4 contributors · 33 comments
  • Followers: 13

Summary

CacheCollector's race condition detection no longer relies on millisecond-precision cache creation timestamps. Instead, it stores a copy of the cache data as it was loaded at the start of the request and computes a content fingerprint at write time to detect whether another process changed the entry in the interim. Two concurrent writes that happen within the same millisecond are now reliably distinguished. State::setMultiple() is also reworked to acquire the collector lock before writing to the key-value store and immediately publish the new value to cache within that lock, closing a related race window in State.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • API change: CacheCollector::$cacheCreated (protected) removed on main and deprecated on 11.x; replaced by $loadedData (nullable array) and the new protected fingerprint(array): string method.
  • Deprecation: CacheCollector::$cacheCreated deprecated on 11.x; subclasses that reference it should switch to $loadedData or rely on the built-in fingerprint comparison in updateCache().

Technical details

The protected $cacheCreated unix-timestamp property is removed from CacheCollector and replaced with $loadedData, a nullable array that lazyLoadCache() populates with a copy of the cache entry's data array when the entry exists. PHP's copy-on-write semantics mean this copy only costs extra memory if the data is subsequently modified, making the approach cheap for read-heavy requests.

A new protected fingerprint(array $data): string method hashes its argument with hash('xxh128', serialize($data)). In updateCache(), the fingerprint of $loadedData is compared against the fingerprint of the cache item's current content. If they match, no other request has touched the entry and the write proceeds; if they differ (or if the data was invalidated during the request), the method backs out rather than overwriting a concurrent request's work. The fingerprint is computed lazily and only when there are actually keys to persist, so purely read-only requests pay no serialization or hashing cost.

Lock acquisition in updateCache() is now decoupled from the safety check: the lock is still attempted to prevent stampedes, but the write decision is governed by the content fingerprint, not by whether the lock was held. When a State::setMultiple() call cannot acquire the lock, it immediately writes an empty tombstone cache item (acting as a signal to concurrent requests) and waits up to one second before retrying the lock. State::set() now delegates to setMultiple() instead of calling parent::set() directly.

The test helper setCacheCreated(int) in the test stub is replaced by setLoadedData(array). New parameterized unit tests cover concurrent-write scenarios including same-millisecond writes that previously defeated the timestamp comparison. On 11.x, $cacheCreated is restored with a deprecation notice to give subclasses a migration path; on main it is removed entirely.

Upgrade

Subclasses of CacheCollector that read or write the protected $cacheCreated property must update. On 11.x the property is deprecated; on main it is removed.

// Before (broken on main, deprecated on 11.x):
$this->cacheCreated = $cache->created;
if ($this->cacheCreated !== $otherCreated) { ... }

// After: rely on the new fingerprint-based detection in updateCache().
// If you need to track whether the cache was loaded, check $this->cacheLoaded
// or compare against $this->loadedData instead.

Test code that called setCacheCreated(int) on the test stub must be updated to call setLoadedData(array) with the data array instead of the timestamp.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: December 28, 2024
  • Committed: June 23, 2026 (1 year and 2 commits later)

Key contributors

  • catch (Third and Grove)
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)
  • godotislate
  • alexpott (Acro Commerce)
  • dww (3281d Consulting)
  • spokje
  • nicxvan (nLightened Development LLC)
  • berdir (MD Systems GmbH)

Collaboration

longwave revived the issue after it sat idle for months. Their first approach (MR!15932) replaced the timestamp with a random token written alongside each cache entry, but they quickly realized that a content hash requires no extra storage at all and is therefore simpler. In the discussion that followed, catch raised the memory cost of keeping a full copy of the data. longwave pointed to PHP's copy-on-write behavior, demonstrating with a live snippet that the copy only allocates additional memory when one of the arrays is actually modified, making the approach essentially free for the common read-heavy case. That observation settled the design. Later, alexpott noticed that subclasses of the canvas module check $this->cacheCreated in lazyLoadCache(), and catch responded by adding a deprecation for the property on 11.x (MR!16122) while removing it outright on main.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3586760: Use composite key Upsert queries in core
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.4.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 16 files, +127/−71 · php +127/−71
  • Diff: 8ba2f05
  • Discussion: #3586760 · 5 contributors · 36 comments
  • Followers: 8

Summary

More than a dozen places in Drupal core that previously used merge() for write-or-update operations now use upsert() with composite keys. The practical effect is that each of these operations no longer issues a preliminary SELECT to decide whether to INSERT or UPDATE; instead a single atomic INSERT ... ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE (or equivalent) is used. Affected paths include session writes, key-value store operations, taxonomy index updates, comment statistics, migration map saves, locale storage, search word weights, shortcut set assignment, inline block usage, and user data storage. Session writes during login drop from 16 to 15 queries per the updated performance tests.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The merge() method in Drupal's database layer performs a preliminary SELECT before deciding between INSERT and UPDATE. The upsert() method, whose composite key support was established in #2547493, bypasses that SELECT entirely with a native single-statement upsert. This change adopts upsert() across the following locations: SessionHandler::doWrite() (sessions table), FileStorage::doWrite() (config), KeyValueStore\DatabaseStorage::doSet() and doSetMultiple(), expirable key-value doSetWithExpire(), CommentStatisticsStorage::update() (comment_entity_statistics), InlineBlockUsage::addUsage() (inline_block_usage), LocaleFileStorage::save() (locale_file), LocaleStringDatabaseStorage::dbStringUpdate() (locales_source / locales_target), SqlWithCentralizedMessageStorage::saveIdMapping() (migration map table), SearchIndexStorage::updateWordWeights() (search_total), ShortcutSetStorage::assignUser() (shortcut_set_users), taxonomy_build_node_index() (taxonomy_index), and UserDataStorage::set() (users_data).

KeyValueStore\DatabaseStorage::setMultiple() received the most substantial rewrite. Previously it delegated to parent::setMultiple() inside a transaction, which internally looped over individual set() calls. The new implementation builds a single multi-row upsert query using ->fields() for the first row and ->values() for each additional row, issuing one statement regardless of how many pairs are written. An early-return guard for an empty $data array was also added, since upsert() does not tolerate an empty value set.

One incidental correctness fix is included: LocaleFileStorage::save() now passes $currentImport->hash ?? '' because upsert()->fields() requires a concrete value for every column while the old merge()->fields() silently omitted a null hash field.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: April 24, 2026
  • First commit: June 19, 2026 (1 month later)
  • Last commit: June 22, 2026 (2 days and 3 commits later)

Key contributors

  • mondrake
  • catch (Tag1 Consulting, Inc.)
  • amateescu
  • alexpott (Acro Commerce)

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3594332: Add a user:login command to generate a one-time login link
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Version: main
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 3 files, +272/−0 · php +272
  • Diff: 761f8a8
  • Discussion: #3594332 · 9 contributors · 27 comments
  • Followers: 12

Summary

Drupal core now ships a user:login console command (alias uli) that generates a one-time login URL for any user account. Site administrators can run vendor/bin/dr user:login to instantly get a login link for user 1, or target a specific account with --uid, --name, or --mail options, and optionally redirect the resulting session to a particular path using the positional path argument. This is one of the top five most-used Drush commands and is now available in core without requiring Drush.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • New API: New user:login (alias uli) console command to generate a one-time login URL, accepting --uid, --name, --mail options and an optional path argument.

Technical details

The command is implemented as Drupal\user\Command\LoginCommand (marked @internal), registered via Symfony Console's #[AsCommand] attribute with the name user:login and alias uli. It accepts an optional positional path argument and three mutually exclusive options (--name, --uid, --mail) for account selection; when none are provided, it falls back to loading user 1. Account lookup uses EntityTypeManagerInterface::getStorage('user')->loadByProperties() and throws \InvalidArgumentException for unresolvable identifiers or blocked accounts. The one-time URL is produced by the existing OneTimeAuthentication::generateOneTimeLoginUrl() service with immediate: TRUE, and the URL string is written to OutputInterface with writeln() (including a trailing newline so the URL does not run into the shell prompt). This is part of Phase 2 of the CLI in Core community initiative. Test coverage is split: a Functional test verifies that the generated link actually authenticates the user in a real browser session, while a Kernel test covers all failure modes (blocked account, unknown uid/name/mail), cutting overall test time roughly in half compared to running every case in a BrowserTestBase.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: June 9, 2026
  • Committed: June 19, 2026 (10 days later)

Key contributors

  • mradcliffe (Kosada)
  • moshe weitzman
  • mstrelan
  • catch (Tag1 Consulting, Inc.)
  • dww (3281d Consulting)
  • heddn (MTech, LLC)
  • godotislate
  • larowlan (PreviousNext)

Collaboration

During review, mradcliffe observed that in Bash the URL printed directly on the same line as the shell prompt because the output lacked a trailing newline (ZSH added one automatically, masking the problem). dww diagnosed the root cause, pushed a commit to restore the newline and add a trim() call in the Functional test to strip it before calling drupalGet(), and the pipeline confirmed the fix. dww also restructured the test suite mid-review, moving all failure-mode cases from BrowserTestBase to a KernelTestBase, which cut the new test suite's execution time from about 18 seconds to about 9 seconds.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3593233: Cloning an aggregate entity query shares its aggregate conditions with the original
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal bug report
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 4 files, +56/−6 · php +56/−6
  • Diff: 3e4104d
  • Discussion: #3593233 · 5 contributors · 16 comments
  • Followers: 6

Summary

Cloning an aggregate entity query now correctly isolates the clone's aggregate condition tree from the original. Before this fix, both the original query and its clone shared the same ConditionAggregate object, so adding conditions to either one silently changed both, and the cloned query could execute SQL built against the wrong query object. The fix mirrors the existing clone handling for regular conditions and also extends ConditionAggregateInterface with setQuery() and getQuery() methods.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • API change: ConditionAggregateInterface gains two new required methods: setQuery(QueryInterface $query): static and getQuery(): QueryInterface.

Technical details

The root cause was in QueryBase::__clone(), which cloned $condition but left $conditionAggregate pointing at the same instance. The SQL-layer Query::__clone() then called $this->condition->setQuery($this) to re-parent the cloned regular condition, but did nothing equivalent for $conditionAggregate.

The fix adds a symmetric clone $this->conditionAggregate call in QueryBase::__clone() (guarded by isset() since non-aggregate queries never set the property), and a matching $this->conditionAggregate->setQuery($this) call in the SQL Query::__clone() override. Because setQuery() was previously absent from ConditionAggregateInterface, the fix also formally adds setQuery(QueryInterface $query): static and getQuery(): QueryInterface to that interface. Both methods are already implemented in ConditionFundamentals, so existing classes that extend it are unaffected at runtime, but any class that implements ConditionAggregateInterface directly without extending ConditionFundamentals must now provide these two methods. A new functional test testCloneAggregateIsolation() in the SQL aggregate query test class covers the before/after behavior.

Upgrade

If you have a custom class that implements ConditionAggregateInterface directly (without extending ConditionFundamentals), add the two new required methods:

public function setQuery(QueryInterface $query): static {
    $this->query = $query;
    return $this;
}

public function getQuery(): QueryInterface {
    return $this->query;
}

Classes that already extend ConditionFundamentals inherit these implementations and need no changes.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: June 2, 2026
  • Committed: June 19, 2026 (17 days and 2 commits later)

Key contributors

  • amateescu
  • godotislate
  • amitgoyal (Acquia)
  • alexpott (Acro Commerce)

Collaboration

amateescu discovered the bug while reviewing #2875033 and noted it is the "sister issue" to #1829942, which they had filed 14 years earlier about non-aggregate query cloning. During MR review, godotislate raised questions about the approach, which led amateescu to add setQuery() and getQuery() formally to ConditionAggregateInterface rather than relying on duck-typing through ConditionFundamentals.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3585723: #date_year_range does not support years < 1000
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal bug report
  • Version: 11.4.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 2 files, +89/−2 · php +89/−2
  • Diff: 4fd9a4d
  • Discussion: #3585723 · 5 contributors · 21 comments
  • Followers: 6

Summary

The #date_year_range form property on date elements silently ignored any absolute year value with fewer than four digits (0 through 999), substituting the current year instead. So a range like 0:2050 or 500:1500 would behave as if the minimum were the current year. This is now corrected: absolute years from 0 to 9999 all work as expected.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The root cause was a too-strict regular expression in DateElementBase::datetimeRangeYears(). The absolute-year pattern was @^[0-9]{4}@, which requires exactly four digits. Any value with fewer digits (0, 1, 500, 999) failed that match and fell through to the relative-offset branch (@[\+|\-][0-9]{1,4}@). Because bare short values like 0 also fail the relative pattern, the method silently fell back to $this_year, producing the wrong result with no warning.

The fix changes the pattern to @^[0-9]{1,4}@, accepting one to four digits as an absolute year. The updated comment clarifies that current-year-relative notation requires an explicit + or - prefix (e.g. +0 for the current year, not 0), and notes that years BC (negative absolute years) remain unsupported due to the sign-prefix ambiguity.

A new unit test class DateElementBaseTest (in core/tests/Drupal/Tests/Core/Datetime/Element/) covers 14 data-driven cases: standard four-digit ranges, years below 1000, year zero, single-digit years, mixed absolute and relative bounds, and both-relative ranges.

Upgrade

If your code passes a #date_year_range value with an absolute year below 1000, you no longer need any workaround. Review any range strings that used a bare 0 intending to mean "current year": they now mean year 0 (1 BCE in the proleptic Gregorian calendar). Use +0 if you want the current year as the boundary.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: April 20, 2026
  • Committed: June 19, 2026 (2 months and 3 commits later)

Key contributors

  • anybody (DROWL.de)
  • gwenweb (WebstanZ)
  • alexpott (Acro Commerce)

Collaboration

gwenweb, a first-time Drupal core contributor, joined the MR opened by anybody and added the missing unit test class together with a typo fix that had been causing CI spell-check failures. After feedback from anybody about phpcs violations and a missing @group annotation, gwenweb updated the MR to use PHP 8 #[Group] and #[DataProvider] attributes, switched to createStub() for PHPUnit 12 compatibility, and fixed code-style issues, getting all 38 CI jobs to pass. anybody then added more test-case combinations before tstoeckler reviewed and approved for merge.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3597406: [regression] DrupalApplication (for `dr`) needs to support DRUPAL_DEV_SITE_PATH env var
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Critical bug report
  • Version: 11.4.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 2 files, +6/−3 · php +6/−3
  • Diff: 09d9f4d
  • Discussion: #3597406 · 3 contributors · 13 comments
  • Followers: 5

Summary

A regression introduced in #3453474 (which added DrupalApplication and deprecated BootableCommandTrait) accidentally dropped support for the DRUPAL_DEV_SITE_PATH environment variable. That variable is the standard way for functional tests to tell the dr command which site directory to use. Without it, recipe-related functional tests in projects such as Drupal CMS could no longer isolate dr to the correct test site. This fix restores the env-var lookup in DrupalApplication and also corrects how RecipeTestTrait invokes dr so that it works correctly in Composer-based installs (such as drupal/core-recommended) rather than only in raw core checkouts.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

In DrupalApplication::bootstrap(), the site path resolution now checks getenv('DRUPAL_DEV_SITE_PATH') first and falls back to DrupalKernel::findSitePath() only when the env var is absent. This mirrors the behaviour that existed in the now-deprecated BootableCommandTrait::getSitePath().

RecipeTestTrait::runDrupalCommand() received a related fix: it previously invoked core/scripts/dr relative to $this->root (the core root). It now uses vendor/bin/dr relative to the Composer project root, retrieved via Composer\InstalledVersions::getRootPackage()['install_path']. This ensures the correct class loader is in place and makes the trait work in installations where core lives under vendor/drupal/core rather than at the repository root.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: June 11, 2026
  • Committed: June 18, 2026 (7 days and 3 commits later)

Key contributors

  • dww (3281d Consulting)
  • phenaproxima (Acquia)
  • moshe
  • mradcliffe (Kosada)
  • catch (Tag1 Consulting, Inc.)

Collaboration

phenaproxima reported the breakage in Slack (they were the original author of BootableCommandTrait and noticed the missing env-var support), then contributed a commit to the MR fixing RecipeTestTrait::runDrupalCommand() to use vendor/bin/dr. dww opened the issue and authored the core DrupalApplication fix, then reviewed phenaproxima's commit. The two effectively reviewed each other's work before marking the MR RTBC. catch merged it urgently because the regression was blocking Drupal CMS testing.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3601433: Fix local_status deprecation by clearing source in a post update
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Critical bug report
  • Version: 11.4.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 2 files, +9/−0 · php +9
  • Diff: 7d0ebcd
  • Discussion: #3601433 · 4 contributors · 14 comments
  • Followers: 5

Summary

Multilingual sites upgrading from Drupal 11.3 to 11.4 could encounter errors in the translation update workflow because locale translation source data stored by existing sites (as plain stdClass objects) was no longer compatible with the LocaleTranslationSource class introduced by the deprecation work in #3590050. A new post-update hook clears that stale source data automatically during the database update process, and the deprecated locale_translation_get_status() shim is fixed to load its required include file before delegating to the new service.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The deprecation of locale_translation_get_status() (see #3590050) moved locale source tracking to the LocaleSource service, which now returns LocaleTranslationSource objects. Sites that had already run a translation check stored plain stdClass objects for locale project data in the database. After upgrading to 11.4 that stored data was loaded back as stdClass but code expected LocaleTranslationSource, causing failures.

The fix adds locale_post_update_clear_sources() in locale.post_update.php, which calls \Drupal::service(LocaleSource::class)->clearSources(). Because this source information is non-persistent (it is rebuilt automatically when stale), clearing it on upgrade is safe and sufficient.

A second bug was also fixed in the deprecated locale_translation_get_status() shim in locale.module: the old function loaded locale.translation.inc as a side effect, but the new shim did not. Tools such as Drush's locale:update command relied on that side effect to make _locale_translation_default_update_options() available. The fix adds an explicit \Drupal::moduleHandler()->loadInclude('locale', 'inc', 'locale.translation') call before the service delegation.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: June 16, 2026
  • Committed: June 18, 2026 (2 days and 3 commits later)

Key contributors

  • berdir (MD Systems GmbH)
  • nicxvan (nLightened Development LLC)
  • catch (Tag1 Consulting, Inc.)

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3590350: WorkspacePublisher doesn't roll back when a PHP Error is thrown during publishing
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Major bug report
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 4 files, +95/−8 · php +95/−8
  • Diff: f239996
  • Discussion: #3590350 · 4 contributors · 13 comments
  • Followers: 4

Summary

Sites using the Workspaces module were exposed to a data-integrity risk: if a PHP \Error subclass (such as \TypeError or \AssertionError) was thrown during a workspace publish or merge operation, the database transaction was not rolled back. This meant the first entities in the save loop could be committed to Live while later ones were not, leaving content in a partially published state with no error logged on the workspaces channel. The fix ensures the transaction is always rolled back and the throwable always logged, regardless of whether an \Exception or an \Error subclass escapes the save loop.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

Both WorkspacePublisher::publish() and WorkspaceMerger::merge() wrapped their entity-save loops in try / catch (\Exception $e) blocks that called $transaction->rollBack() and Error::logException() before rethrowing. Because PHP's \Error hierarchy is not a subclass of \Exception, any \Error thrown from inside the closure (for example from a hook_entity_presave() implementation or a downstream service called during $entity->save()) bypassed the catch block entirely. The fix widens both catch clauses to catch (\Throwable $e). Error::logException() already accepts \Throwable, so no change to the catch body was required. A PHPStan suppression for a false-positive isset.variable warning in WorkspaceMerger.php was also removed as it became unnecessary. A kernel test (WorkspacePublisherTest::testPublishThrowableRollback) was added using a #[Hook('node_presave')] implementation on the test class itself to simulate a failure mid-loop; a data provider covers both \RuntimeException (previously caught) and \Error (previously missed), asserting that neither node's Live revision changes and that the error is recorded on the workspaces logger channel.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: May 15, 2026
  • Committed: June 15, 2026 (1 month and 2 commits later)

Key contributors

  • amateescu
  • godotislate

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3524377: Allow to skip OOP hooks and services for modules that are not installed
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 9 files, +206/−6 · php +201/−6 · yml +5
  • Diff: 11e9b82
  • Discussion: #3524377 · 11 contributors · 90 comments
  • Followers: 20

Summary

Drupal now provides a #[HookDependsOnModule] PHP attribute that module developers can place on an OOP hook class or method to make registration conditional on another module being installed. When the named module is absent, the annotated hook method (or entire class) is skipped at container build time. If every hook method in a class is skipped, the class is not added to the container at all. This solves two problems: it avoids unnecessary service registrations for hooks that can never fire, and it lets developers declare service-container dependencies as required constructor arguments instead of nullable ones, because the hook class will simply never be instantiated when the required module is absent.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • New API: New #[HookDependsOnModule] attribute for conditionally registering OOP hook classes or methods based on whether a module is installed.

Technical details

The new Drupal\Core\Hook\Attribute\HookDependsOnModule class is a PHP attribute declared with TARGET_CLASS | TARGET_METHOD | IS_REPEATABLE, accepts a single $module string, and implements HookAttributeInterface. The check is performed inside HookCollectorPass::collectModuleHookImplementations() against $this->modules, the list of installed modules already available in the pass. Class-level attributes are now also serialized into the file cache entry (as class_attributes) so the check is fast on warm runs.

The logic runs in two stages: first a loop over class-level HookDependsOnModule instances uses continue 2 to skip the entire file if any dependency is unmet; then a per-method inner loop does the same for method-level instances. A hook class with no surviving method registrations is never added to the container.

Two in-tree examples land in the same commit. FileViewsHooks receives #[HookDependsOnModule('views')] on the class, which lets the formerly nullable ?FieldViewsDataProvider constructor argument become required. MenuUiHooks::menuDelete() receives #[HookDependsOnModule('node')] on the method, removing a runtime $this->entityTypeManager->hasDefinition('node_type') guard that was previously needed to handle the case where node module was absent.

Upgrade

Module developers with OOP hook classes that inject services from an optionally-installed module can now declare those dependencies as required and add the attribute:

use Drupal\Core\Hook\Attribute\Hook;
use Drupal\Core\Hook\Attribute\HookDependsOnModule;

// Class-level: skip the entire class when 'search' is not installed.
#[HookDependsOnModule('search')]
class NodeSearchHooks {
  public function __construct(
    protected readonly SearchIndexInterface $searchIndex, // now required, not nullable
  ) {}

  #[Hook('cron')]
  public function cron(): void { ... }
}

// Method-level: skip only this method when 'node' is not installed.
#[Hook('menu_delete')]
#[HookDependsOnModule('node')]
public function menuDelete(EntityInterface $entity): void { ... }

The attribute is repeatable; if any named module is absent the class or method is skipped. Because a non-existent attribute class is not a fatal error (unlike a non-existent named parameter on an existing attribute), contrib modules can safely use #[HookDependsOnModule] and simply declare a minimum core version requirement for the class to be recognized.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: May 14, 2025
  • Committed: June 15, 2026 (1 year and 2 commits later)

Key contributors

  • berdir (MD Systems GmbH)
  • kristiaanvandeneynde (Factorial.io)
  • nicxvan (nLightened Development LLC)
  • catch (Third and Grove)
  • acbramley (PreviousNext)
  • godotislate

Collaboration

berdir opened the issue and drove the implementation. A key design debate in the thread concerned whether to add a named parameter to the existing #[Hook] attribute (or a generic ConditionalRegister attribute) versus introducing a dedicated class. berdir's argument, confirmed with a live code snippet at https://3v4l.org/31a3W, was that using a non-existent named parameter on an existing attribute is a PHP fatal error, making a separate attribute class the only approach that is safe across the multi-version contrib compatibility window. kristiaanvandeneynde pushed back in favor of a more generic name to leave room for future dependency types (themes, callbacks), and berdir and nicxvan countered that purpose-specific attribute classes are already precedent in the hook system and that any future use case can get its own attribute without BC concerns. acbramley did most of the rebase and cleanup work in the final stretch, renaming the constructor parameter to $module for consistency with #[Hook] and applying godotislate's doc-review suggestions before the commit landed.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3400181: [regression] calling TypedConfigManager::getDefinition() causes cache pollution
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Major bug report
  • Version: 11.4.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 2 files, +3/−22 · php +3/−22
  • Diff: 39c1792
  • Discussion: #3400181 · 7 contributors · 21 comments
  • Followers: 13

Summary

A regression in TypedConfigManager caused config schema definitions to be stored incorrectly in the in-memory cache when the double-bracket (||) composite type syntax was used. Once the cache was polluted by a call to getDefinition(), subsequent typed config lookups for the same type could resolve against the wrong (incomplete) definition, leading to silent schema mismatches. This bug was severe enough that at least one contributed module (Canvas) had to override the config.typed service entirely to work around it.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The root cause was introduced by #2663410. TypedConfigManager::getDefinitionWithReplacements() handles a special edge case where a schema type uses the || separator (e.g. wrapping.test.double_brackets.*||test.double_brackets.cat.dog). When this edge case fires, the method processes the composite type and then unconditionally wrote the result to $this->definitions[$type]. The problem: getDefinition() calls getDefinitionWithReplacements() without passing any $replacements, so this unconditional write stored an un-replaced, incomplete definition in the cache. Any subsequent call that did pass replacements would then hit the stale cache entry and skip the replacement logic entirely.

The fix in TypedConfigManager.php adds a single guard: if (!empty($replacements)) { $this->definitions[$type] = $definition; }. Only calls that actually performed replacements are now allowed to populate the cache entry. Three clearCachedDefinitions() workaround calls in ConfigSchemaTest::testConfigSaveWithWrappingSchemaDoubleBrackets(), each accompanied by a @todo Remove in #3400181 annotation, are removed as a direct consequence.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: November 8, 2023
  • Committed: June 14, 2026 (2 years and 3 commits later)

Key contributors

  • wim leers (Acquia)
  • claudiu.cristea (Webikon)
  • huzooka (Munyu)
  • dimilias (European Commission and European Union Institutions, Agencies and Bodies)
  • alexpott (Acro Commerce)
  • herved (European Commission and European Union Institutions, Agencies and Bodies)
  • catch (Tag1 Consulting, Inc.)

Collaboration

wim leers filed the issue in November 2023 and identified both the regressing commit (#2663410) and the exact code path responsible. Over a year and a half later, huzooka submitted the MR after the Canvas project (#3568264) ran into the bug in a real-world schema and had to override the core service as a workaround. huzooka's comment in the MR noted that removing the existing clearCachedDefinitions() calls from the test was sufficient test coverage, a point claudiu.cristea seconded, sparing the need for new test authorship.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3408420: Deleting a workflow can lead to the deletion of unaffected views
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Critical bug report
  • Version: main
  • Status: Fixed
  • Discussion: #3408420 · 4 contributors · 17 comments
  • Followers: 5

Summary

Deleting a workflow could silently delete views and other configuration entities that had no real dependency on the workflow. The same underlying defect could also cause a user role to lose a permission when an unrelated field was removed from a content type with Layout Builder enabled. Both symptoms were caused by a dependency-ordering bug in the configuration system that made config entities get processed in the wrong sequence during a cascading deletion. The fix ensures that the dependency processing order is preserved correctly, so only configuration that genuinely depends on the deleted item is affected.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The defect was in ConfigManager::findConfigEntityDependenciesAsEntities(). That method calls findConfigEntityDependencies() to get an ordered list of config names (ordered so that dependents come before their dependencies), then loads the corresponding entities using EntityStorageInterface::loadMultiple() grouped by entity type. The problem: loadMultiple() returns entities sorted by their own storage order, not by the dependency order. This silently reordered the returned entity list. getConfigEntitiesToChangeOnDependencyRemoval() processes the list from the end, relying on the ordering to ensure a dependency is processed before any entity that depends on it. When the order was wrong, a role could be processed before an entity view display it depended on. Because that display appeared to be in the removal list, the system incorrectly stripped the role's permission.

The fix pre-populates the result array with keys in the correct dependency order using array_fill_keys(array_keys($dependencies), NULL), then fills in each loaded entity by its config dependency name ($loaded_entity->getConfigDependencyName()). A final array_filter() removes any entries that were not loaded. This preserves the order from findConfigEntityDependencies() regardless of how loadMultiple() sorts entities internally.

The fix was applied in the related issue #3504368 and committed to 10.5.x, 11.1.x, and 11.x. Issue #3408420 was subsequently closed because the same ordering fix resolved the workflow-deletion-deletes-views scenario.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: December 13, 2023

Collaboration

The root cause proved elusive: the alphabetical name of a contributing module changed which config objects were in the static cache on first load versus subsequent loads, altering the order findConfigEntityDependencies() returned, which meant the bug was not reliably reproducible in a clean install. godotislate isolated the mechanism by tracing the list order through findConfigEntityDependenciesAsEntities() and identifying how grouping by entity type for loadMultiple() destroyed the dependency order. godotislate then proposed and implemented the fix in MR 11141, while dpagini confirmed the fix resolved the original project issue.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3599842: guzzlehttp/psr7 needs to be updated to >2.10.2 to fix 2 security issues
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Critical bug report
  • Version: 10.6.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 3 files, +13/−12 · lock +11/−10 · json +2/−2
  • Diff: 833a440
  • Discussion: #3599842 · 6 contributors · 24 comments
  • Followers: 22

Summary

Two medium-severity security vulnerabilities in the guzzlehttp/psr7 library (CVE-2026-48998, Host Confusion via Authority Reinterpretation; and CVE-2026-49214, CRLF Injection via URI Host Component) were publicly registered on June 11 2026 against versions below 2.10.2. Drupal core's drupal/core-recommended package was pinning ~2.8.0, which prevented composer update from pulling in a safe release and caused composer audit to fail on any project using that metapackage. This change raises the minimum to ^2.10.2 (resolved to 2.10.4 in the lock file) across the affected branches, so sites can install a clean dependency tree without workarounds.

Impact

Affects site owners.

  • Security: Two medium-severity CVEs in guzzlehttp/psr7 (<2.10.2): CVE-2026-48998 (Host Confusion via Authority Reinterpretation) and CVE-2026-49214 (CRLF Injection via URI Host Component).
  • Requirements: Minimum guzzlehttp/psr7 raised from ~2.8.0 to ~2.10.4 in drupal/core-recommended.

Technical details

The constraint for guzzlehttp/psr7 was updated in three places within the single commit on main (with parallel commits on 11.3.x and 10.6.x): composer.json at the repo root changed from ^2.8.0 to ^2.10.2, core/composer.json (used by drupal/core-recommended) changed its pinned tilde constraint from ~2.8.0 to ~2.10.4, and composer.lock was regenerated to record guzzlehttp/psr7 at 2.10.4. The 11.4.x and 11.x branches were already on 2.10.4 and needed no changes. The contributor noted that bumping to 2.11.0 introduced deprecations, so 2.10.4 was chosen as the target instead. The two CVEs affect URI parsing: GHSA-hq7v-mx3g-29hw covers CRLF injection via the URI host component, and GHSA-34xg-wgjx-8xph covers host confusion via authority reinterpretation.

Upgrade

Sites running drupal/recommended-project on affected branches (main, 11.3.x, 10.6.x) should update to the patched Drupal core release and run composer update. Before the patched release is available, the workaround is:

composer require "guzzlehttp/psr7":"2.10.4 as 2.8.1"

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: June 12, 2026
  • Committed: June 13, 2026

Key contributors

  • deviantintegral
  • godotislate
  • dww (3281d Consulting)
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3585891: Deprecate Validating CSRF tokens with the 'rest' key in CsrfRequestHeaderAccessCheck
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.4.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 2 files, +9/−6 · php +9/−6
  • Diff: 41b8957
  • Discussion: #3585891 · 6 contributors · 25 comments
  • Followers: 6

Summary

Drupal 11.4 deprecates the fallback that allowed CSRF tokens validated against the old 'rest' key to pass the X-CSRF-Token access check. This key was a leftover from a Drupal 8 migration path and a @todo comment in the code targeted its removal as far back as Drupal 8.3. Any module that still generates or validates CSRF tokens using the literal string 'rest' will now trigger a deprecation notice; the behaviour continues working but will break in Drupal 12. The user.login.http endpoint was also updated to generate its CSRF token using the canonical key.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • Deprecation: CSRF token validation and generation using the hardcoded 'rest' key is deprecated in 11.4.0; switch callers to CsrfRequestHeaderAccessCheck::TOKEN_KEY before Drupal 12.0.0

Technical details

In CsrfRequestHeaderAccessCheck::access(), the old double-validate() call (checking both self::TOKEN_KEY and 'rest') has been restructured: if the primary check fails and the token validates under 'rest', a @trigger_error() deprecation is issued but the request is still allowed through. If neither key validates, AccessResult::forbidden() is returned as before. The constant TOKEN_KEY is 'X-CSRF-Token'.

UserAuthenticationController::login() previously called $this->csrfToken->get('rest') to produce the CSRF token included in the login response. It now imports CsrfRequestHeaderAccessCheck and calls $this->csrfToken->get(CsrfRequestHeaderAccessCheck::TOKEN_KEY). This was the concrete caller that caused initial test failures when a straight removal was attempted, and it motivated pivoting to a deprecation-first approach rather than an immediate removal.

Upgrade

Any code that calls $csrfToken->get('rest') to produce a CSRF token for REST requests must switch to the constant:

// Before (deprecated):
$token = $this->csrfToken->get('rest');

// After:
use Drupal\Core\Access\CsrfRequestHeaderAccessCheck;
$token = $this->csrfToken->get(CsrfRequestHeaderAccessCheck::TOKEN_KEY);

Similarly, any explicit $csrfToken->validate($value, 'rest') calls should use CsrfRequestHeaderAccessCheck::TOKEN_KEY as the second argument. The 'rest' key fallback is removed in Drupal 12.0.0.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: April 20, 2026
  • Committed: June 12, 2026 (1 month and 3 commits later)

Key contributors

  • sourav_paul (Innoraft)
  • smustgrave (Mobomo)
  • macsim
  • godotislate
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)

Collaboration

prudloff noted in the thread that tests were failing when the removal was attempted outright because UserAuthenticationController was still producing tokens with the 'rest' key, and that contrib modules could be doing the same. This observation prompted the switch from direct removal to a formal deprecation cycle.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3593963: Field loading can hit the MySQL 61 table join limit if there are ~60 fields
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Critical bug report
  • Version: 11.4.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 2 files, +320/−100 · php +320/−100
  • Diff: 7d3b034
  • Discussion: #3593963 · 7 contributors · 41 comments
  • Followers: 15

Summary

Sites with content types that define roughly 60 or more fields can hit a fatal database error when loading entities, because MySQL and MariaDB enforce a hard limit of 61 tables per JOIN and SQLite has a comparable limit. The error surfaced in Drupal 11.4.x after earlier optimizations consolidated field loading into single queries that JOIN each dedicated field storage table. After this fix, entity loading on sites with large numbers of fields works correctly again regardless of how many fields are defined.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The problem was introduced by #3564689 and #3551308, which changed SqlContentEntityStorage::loadFromDedicatedTables() to load all single-cardinality fields in one query and all multiple-cardinality fields in another, each using a LEFT JOIN per field. With ~60 or more fields that use dedicated storage tables, the number of joins exceeds MySQL's documented limit of 61.

The fix adds a constant FIELD_MINIMUM_CHUNK_SIZE = 25 to SqlContentEntityStorage and splits both the single-cardinality and multiple-cardinality field lists into chunks before querying. The chunking logic merges the last chunk into the penultimate one, so 26 fields load as a single chunk of 26 (not 25 + 1) and 51 fields load as chunks of 25 and 26. This keeps each query well under database limits while avoiding an excessively small final batch.

The inline field-loading code was extracted into two new private methods, loadSingleCardinalityFields() and loadMultipleCardinalityFields(), which are called in a foreach loop over the chunks. A $load_shared_table_fields boolean is passed to loadSingleCardinalityFields() and is only true for the first chunk, preventing shared base/revision-data-table fields from being fetched and overwritten on subsequent iterations. A new kernel test testEntityWithManyFieldsLoad() in SqlContentEntityStorageTest creates 71 fields on entity_test_rev and asserts all field values survive a save/load and save/loadRevision cycle for both single and unlimited cardinality.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: June 5, 2026
  • Committed: June 10, 2026 (4 days and 3 commits later)

Key contributors

  • smustgrave (Mobomo)
  • godotislate
  • catch (Tag1 Consulting, Inc.)
  • joachim (Chicken)
  • alexpott (Acro Commerce)

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3590897: Sidebar toggle is visible and functioning in media library dialog, but shouldn't be there
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal bug report
  • Version: 11.4.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 1 file, +1/−12 · php +1/−12
  • Diff: 05482f5
  • Discussion: #3590897 · 4 contributors · 16 comments
  • Followers: 5

Summary

When using the default admin theme and clicking the media browser on a media reference field, the sidebar toggle button was incorrectly rendered inside the media library dialog. This allowed users to trigger main-page navigation from within the overlay. The fix removes the spurious button from all AJAX-driven dialogs, including media library popups opened via media reference fields.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The problem was in FormHooks::stickyActionButtonsAndSidebar() in the default admin theme. The early-return guard that prevents injecting the sidebar toggle and sticky action button markup into dialogs used a private isModalOrOffcanvas() helper. That helper checked the _wrapper_format request parameter for drupal_modal or drupal_dialog substrings. However, the media library opened from a media reference field sends _wrapper_format=drupal_ajax, which does not match either string, so the guard never fired and the sidebar toggle was included in the form output.

The fix replaces the call to the now-deleted isModalOrOffcanvas() with a call to the existing isAjax() helper. Because the default admin theme does not support sticky action buttons in any AJAX context, the broader isAjax() guard is the correct check. The isModalOrOffcanvas() private method was removed entirely.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: May 19, 2026
  • Committed: June 10, 2026 (21 days and 3 commits later)

Key contributors

  • jurgenhaas (LakeDrops)
  • mherchel (Dripyard)
  • bernardm28

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3592497: Buttons are not rendered on the bottom of the page for Node create/edit for
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Major bug report
  • Version: 11.4.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 1 file, +4/−1 · twig +4/−1
  • Diff: a81450d
  • Discussion: #3592497 · 6 contributors · 19 comments
  • Followers: 9

Summary

On the node create and edit form, the Save and Preview action buttons were rendering immediately below the Title field instead of at the bottom of the page when using Drupal's new default admin theme (introduced in 11.x). This has been fixed; the buttons now appear at the bottom of the form as expected.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The template responsible for the node edit form layout in the new default admin theme was passing the wrong key names to the form|without(...) Twig filter. It excluded gin_actions and gin_sidebar_toggle, which are Gin-theme identifiers that do not exist in Drupal core's node form, so the actual actions element was never suppressed from the main region. It was therefore rendered inline, right after the title field.

The fix has two parts. First, the exclusion list in form|without(...) is corrected: gin_actions becomes actions and gin_sidebar_toggle becomes default_admin_sidebar_toggle. Second, a new <div class="layout-region layout-region-node-footer"> block is added after the secondary (sidebar) region and explicitly renders {{ form.actions }} there, placing the buttons at the bottom of the form where they belong.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: May 28, 2026
  • Committed: June 9, 2026 (12 days and 3 commits later)

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3590364: Part 2 of Consolidate, refactor, remove and untangle Claro's CSS from Default Admin theme
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: main
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 92 files, +4828/−9917 · css +4793/−9798 · yml +28/−118 · svg +6 · js +1/−1
  • Diff: 3056dde
  • Discussion: #3590364 · 8 contributors · 68 comments
  • Followers: 9

Summary

This issue completes the second phase of CSS consolidation for the new default_admin admin theme (see #3582833 for Part 1). The remaining migration CSS files inherited from Gin and Claro have been merged into the theme's permanent stylesheet locations and the migration directory stubs deleted. Along the way, several visual improvements and bug fixes landed: rounded corner radii are now sharper (--base-border-radius changed from 0.5rem to 2px), the header background shifts to a bluer tone, media library and dialog layouts were repaired, and dark-mode support was added or fixed in several components. The autocomplete loading indicator was redesigned from a background-image SVG to a CSS animation spinner. Any site using the default_admin theme will see these visual changes automatically after updating to 11.4.

Impact

Affects front-end developers.

  • Theme change: .claro-autocomplete renamed to .gin-autocomplete; .project__action_button dropped from .button rule; --base-border-radius changed from 0.5rem to 2px; .button now uses display: inline-flex instead of inline-block

Technical details

The migration directory core/themes/default_admin/migration/css/ held Gin-origin stylesheet stubs for components, layout, and theme overrides. This issue merged all remaining stubs into the canonical css/ tree and removed the stubs.

Key mechanical changes visible in the diff: --base-border-radius was corrected from 0.5rem to 2px and --gin-bg-header changed from #eeeff3 to #e1eafc in variables.pcss.css. --vertical-tabs-border-color was fixed to reference --gin-border-color instead of the nonexistent --details-border-color. The .button rule switched from display: inline-block to display: inline-flex; align-items: center; box-sizing: border-box, and the .project__action_button selector was dropped from that rule entirely. In autocomplete-loading.module.pcss.css, the Claro-origin wrapper class .claro-autocomplete and its sibling .claro-autocomplete__message were renamed to .gin-autocomplete and .gin-autocomplete__message; the SVG spinner background-image was replaced with a CSS @keyframes gin-throbber animation. The CKEditor 5 integration file, which previously contained only a single --ck-color-base-border override, now maps the full suite of --ck-* custom properties to --gin-* design tokens. New standalone files were created for components that had no Claro equivalent: description-toggle, contextual-links, edit-form, revisions, settings, sidebar, and status. Details and dialog components gained dark-mode rules and logical-property rewrites. Toolbar integration CSS was removed because the toolbar module is being deprecated for Drupal 12.

Upgrade

Contrib modules or custom CSS that target .claro-autocomplete or .claro-autocomplete__message must be updated:

/* Before */
.claro-autocomplete { … }
.claro-autocomplete__message { … }

/* After */
.gin-autocomplete { … }
.gin-autocomplete__message { … }

The .project__action_button selector no longer inherits .button styles from default_admin. If a module relied on this for button appearance in the admin theme, add the .button class directly to the element or target .project__action_button explicitly in your own stylesheet.

Custom CSS that references --base-border-radius expecting 0.5rem rounded corners will now receive 2px. Override the variable in your sub-theme if the old value is required.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: May 15, 2026
  • Committed: June 9, 2026 (25 days and 3 commits later)

Key contributors

  • mherchel (Dripyard)
  • bernardm28
  • dharizza (Evolving Web)
  • mukeysh (gai Technologies Pvt Ltd)
  • jurgenhaas (LakeDrops)

Collaboration

mherchel drove the bulk of the work and documented design decisions as they went: noting that dialog CSS contained an entire copy of form styles that could be removed, catching that the autocomplete_deluxe contrib module had bespoke overrides baked into core (opening a separate issue), and flagging that mukeysh's first contributions accidentally committed plain CSS instead of PCSS and broke image paths. dharizza completed maintenance-page, breadcrumb, sidebar, node-preview, tableselect, navigation, and top-bar. bernardm28 contributed status component work and provided Safari-specific QA feedback that identified two rendering issues, one of which was already being tracked at #3590897. jurgenhaas performed an independent full manual review of all admin pages against both the unpatched branch and Gin 5 on Drupal 11, confirmed no regressions, and documented remaining visual differences in a separate tracking issue.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.
